segments.title: History as a source for science-fiction realism
segments.summary: A conversation with Glynn Stewart connects historical reading, character-driven decision making, logistics, and command realism to science-fiction writing.
segments.body: The interview treats history as more than background flavor for science fiction. Dr. Clarke praises Stewart's characters because their choices carry historical weight, then the discussion moves through historical reading, ship-maneuver math, logistics, trust, and command failure as practical tools for making fictional military worlds feel plausible. The exchange shows how Dr. Clarke applies naval-history habits outside a conventional lecture format.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke frames history as character-driven and uses Stewart's military science fiction to discuss decisions that remain morally consequential after survival-driven choices.
The conversation links Stewart's character notes, ship-maneuver spreadsheets, and recent historical reading to the craft behind the books.
The later exchange connects history and science fiction through trust, loyalty, cynicism, and the critique of hard-decision command tropes.
